title: Q: Risk, rents, or growth?
abstract: We document that the increasing polarization in Tobin’s Q within industries is closely connected to the growing divergence in rents and the emergence of superstar firms over the past four decades, while discount rates and growth rates did not exhibit the same increasing dispersion. We explain these industry polarization trends in an estimated general equilibrium model where each industry consists of large superstar oligopolists and small monopolistically competitive firms with endogenous transitions between them. Small firms make investments in speculative innovation to increase their probability of becoming superstars. Our model estimation finds that rising entry barriers in both small and superstar firms contribute to rising polarization in markups, but the rising barriers to creating small firms and increasing tastes for goods produced by superstars account for most of the divergence in Q. Stunting the creation of small firms generates greater incentives for speculative innovation, magnifying the impact of market power dispersion on industry polarization in Q.

author.Alexandre Corhay: Rotman School of Management, University of Toronto, 105 St. George Street, Toronto, M5S 3E6, ON, Canada
author.Howard Kung: London Business School and CEPR, Regent’s Park, Sussex Place, London, NW1 4SA, United Kingdom
author.Lukas Schmid*: Marshall School of Business, USC and CEPR, 701 Exposition Blvd, Los Angeles, 90089, CA, USA

article.1. Introduction: Aggregate stock market valuations relative to the replacement value of physical capital (Tobin’s Q) soared to unprecedented heights since the early 2000s despite a slowdown in productivity and investment rates. Recent literature links the divergence between aggregate fundamentals and Q to the rising average market power of firms (e.g., Farhi and Gourio (2018), Eggertsson et al. (2021), and Gutiérrez et al. (2021)). We find that the emergence of a few dominant firms (“superstars”) in each industry drives the surge in aggregate Q, also leading to increasing polarization. The rise of superstars over the past two decades also accounts for the secular trends of increasing market power and declining labor shares (e.g., Van Reenen (2018), De Loecker et al. (2020) and Autor et al. (2020)).We exploit the forward-looking nature of Q to identify the sources of inequality within industries as we can link Q to the market expectations of the future path of markups, profitability, intangible capital shares, discount rates, and growth rates using identities. Fig. 1 plots these variables for superstars and the rest of the firms in an industry. We define superstars as firms in the top 5th percentile of market capitalization in each industry and small firms as the remaining firms.1 This figure shows that Q, markups, profitability, and the intangible capital share diverged between superstars and the rest of the industry’s firms from 1978 to 2019, while sales growth and discount rates did not.2 We define polarization as the difference in average firm characteristics between superstars and small firms within industries. This figure suggests that the rising polarization in Q is related to superstars’ increasing rents and intangible capital share.We aim to explain the industry polarization trends depicted in Fig. 1 using a general equilibrium model featuring an endogenous industry market structure of superstars and small firms. Superstars are oligopolists that strategically compete on prices of differentiated superstar goods and use intangible capital accumulated from fundamental innovation in production. Positive spillovers from superstar intangible capital generate sustained long-term productivity growth. Small firms are monopolistically competitive firms that produce differentiated goods and engage in speculative innovation that positively affects their probability of making a breakthrough to become a superstar. There is a competitive fringe of startups that are required to pay an upfront fixed cost to enter an industry as a small firm. The endogenous transitions between small firms and superstars produce rich variation in the distribution of market power within industries.Download: Download high-res image (451KB)Download: Download full-size imageFig. 1. Industry Polarization.This figure plots the 5-year centered moving average of Tobin’s Q, markups, profitability, intangible capital shares, sales growth, and discount rates for 1978–2019. The variable definitions are provided in internet appendix C.2. We aggregate small and superstar firms across industries and then take a rolling five-year, centered moving average. Superstars are defined as the top 5% ranked by market cap in each 4-digit NAICS industry. Small firms are defined as the remaining firms, excluding Superstars.We estimate the model in two subsamples,1978–1998 and 1999–2019, to identify the economic drivers of the growing inequality in Q, rents, and intangible capital between superstars and the rest of the firms, but without generating polarization in discount rates or sales growth. We use a wide array of industry polarization and aggregate trends to identify the structural parameters in each subsample. We derive a Q decomposition that relates the differences in Q between superstars and small firms to differences in markups, sales to capital, intangible capital shares, discount rates, exit rates, fixed costs, and growth rates. Guided by the structural decomposition, we perform comparative statics highlighting how the model parameters contribute to rising Q polarization.Our estimation results find that increasing barriers to becoming a superstar and to entering as a small firm jointly account for most of the increasing gap between superstar markups and the rest of the firms in the industry. Indeed, in the model, the equilibrium superstar markup is increasing with respect to the sales share of an individual superstar relative to the entire industry (small firms and superstars). Increasing barriers for a startup to enter as a small firm reduces the mass of small firms, thereby increasing the relative sales share of an individual superstar. Rising barriers to entry as a superstar reduce the number of superstars, which also raises the relative sales share of an individual superstar. Therefore, rising barriers to entry at each stage of the life cycle contribute to rising differences in superstar markups relative to other firms by consolidating the market share of incumbent superstars, jointly explaining around 75% of the total increase in markup polarization. Increasing demand for superstar goods accounts for most of the remaining markup differences.While the two sources of barriers to entry contribute similarly to industry polarization in markups, they have very different effects on Q polarization. We find that increasing barriers to becoming a small firm explain over a third of the total rise in Q polarization. In contrast, the increasing barriers to becoming a superstar have a negligible effect on Q polarization. A primary reason for the contrasting effects on Q is how the barriers at different stages affect speculative innovation incentives of small firms in opposite directions, which then affects the level of technology and the intensity of the knowledge spillovers from superstar intangible capital accumulated from fundamental innovation.Higher barriers for a startup to become a small firm increase the relative market power of superstars, making it more attractive for a small firm to become a superstar to capture the higher expected rents. This leads small firms to engage in more speculative innovation to increase their likelihood of becoming superstars. Increasing the number of superstars enhances the positive spillover effects of fundamental innovation on technology, boosting trend growth. Higher growth magnifies the effects of higher superstar markups on Q polarization by lowering the growth-adjusted discount rate in the present value of rents.In contrast, raising the barriers to entry to becoming a superstar discourages speculative innovation by lowering the likelihood of successful transitions as superstars. Superstars consolidate their market power with fewer competitors by charging higher markups and cutting production. The fall in superstar production reduces demand for fundamental innovation, lowering trend growth. Lower growth dampens the effects of higher superstar markups on Q by increasing the growth-adjusted discount rate in the present value of rents. Therefore, polarization in Q is highly informative about barriers at different stages in the life cycle of firms because it reflects expectations of how the innovation margins impact the valuation of future rents.Rising demand for superstar goods and an increasing reliance on intangible capital in superstar production technology each account for around one-third of the rise in Q polarization, akin to the contribution from the rising barriers to becoming small firms. These three channels endogenously interact to reinforce the acceleration in Q polarization in the second half of our sample. The rising barriers and increasing preference for superstar goods make the demand for superstar goods more inelastic, increasing the expected future superstar rents. Increasing the share of intangible capital in superstar production technology increases Q directly because we define Q as the market value of the firm relative to physical capital and indirectly through the endogenous growth channel. Higher superstar intangible capital raises trend growth due to knowledge spillovers. Higher trend growth lowers the growth-adjusted discount rate and increases the present value of future rents.We examine several policy implications of our model to illustrate how pro-competitive regulation at different stages (i.e., encouraging the creation of new firms versus directly curbing the market power of superstars) can have disparate consequences for industry inequality and aggregate growth. While lowering barriers to entry as a small firm and as a superstar would help reverse the rising inequality in market power, only the former would reduce the widening gap in Q between superstars and the rest of the firms. However, encouraging the entry of new small firms weakens superstar rents and discourages speculative innovation, leading to fewer superstars. A small number of superstars weakens the positive spillover effects of fundamental innovation, generating a slowdown in trend growth.In contrast, reducing the barriers to becoming a superstar stimulates more speculative innovation, enhancing the spillover effects of fundamental innovation and boosting trend growth. Our policy experiments suggest that pro-competitive policies targeting superstars versus new firms distort innovation incentives and affect trend growth in opposite directions. They also caution against policies targeting inequality in valuation ratios like Tobin’s Q. Directly curbing the market power of superstars reduces inequality in markups but not in Q while stimulating growth. Encouraging the creation of new small firms reduces inequality in markups and Q but dampens aggregate growth in our model. In short, accounting for a mixed industry structure is important for analyzing competition policy.Our paper connects to the broad literature examining the drivers and implications of macroeconomic trends in the past few decades. Existing research documents secular trends in interest rates (Caballero et al., 2017), the labor share (Barkai (2020), Karabarbounis and Neiman (2016), Rognlie (2015), Hartman-Glaser et al. (2019), Eisfeldt et al. (2023), and Greenwald et al. (2024)), Tobin’s Q (Gutiérrez and Philippon (2016), Eggertsson et al. (2021) and Farhi and Gourio (2018)), investment-Q relation (Andrei et al., 2019), changes in competition ((Gutiérrez and Philippon, 2016), Grullon et al. (2019), and De Loecker et al. (2020)), and intangible investment (Crouzet and Eberly (2018), Crouzet and Eberly (2019)). Our paper shows how some of these trends are driven by superstars and related to increasing industry polarization. We focus on showing how the increasing polarization in Q within industries is driven by the growing inequality in markups, rents, and intangible capital using an estimated general equilibrium model with mixed industries.We relate to studies highlighting the growing impact of superstars on industries and the macroeconomy. There are papers showing that the secular rise in markups (De Loecker et al., 2020) across industries, the decline in the aggregate labor share (Autor et al., 2020), and rising sales concentration (Van Reenen, 2018) are driven by superstars. Liu et al. (2022) show that low-interest rate environments can induce higher market concentration. We complement these papers by documenting that the increasing trend in Tobin’s Q is driven by superstars, reflecting the growing inequality in rents and intangible capital within industries. Cavenaile et al. (2019) emphasize the role of antitrust policies for determining the endogenous industry structure in an estimated model of superstars and small firms with endogenous growth and M&A. We differ by focusing our estimation on explaining the increasing polarization in Q over our two sample periods. Ayyagari et al. (2024) find that accounting for intangible capital in the return on invested capital (ROIC) explains most of the growing differences in ROIC between superstars and small firms.3 We focus on documenting and explaining the increasing polarization in Q. While we find that intangible capital plays an important role in explaining the rising polarization in Q between superstars and small firms, our structural estimation shows that increasing differences in markups and sales are equally important as intangible capital in contributing to the polarization in Q.Our model elements of endogenous entry, imperfect competition, and innovation connect our paper to the broader literature examining the asset pricing implications of firms’ intangible capital (Ai et al. (2013) and Crouzet and Eberly (2023)), product market imperfections (Corhay (2017), Lyandres and Palazzo (2016), Loualiche (2021), Opp et al. (2014), Iraola and Santos (2017), Dou et al. (2021), Wang et al. (2022), Neuhann and Sockin (2020), and Chen et al. (2024)), and endogenous growth margins to generate long-run risks (Kung (2015), Kung and Schmid (2015), and Corhay et al. (2020)). In contrast to these papers, we focus on documenting and identifying the drivers of the growing inequality in Q in an estimated model with superstar and small firms.
article.2. Model: We build a dynamic stochastic general equilibrium model featuring industries populated with superstar firms and small firms to explain the polarization trends within industries. We assume that markets are complete. A representative household accumulates the physical and R&D capital stocks in the economy.Production consists of two tiers (e.g., Jaimovich and Floetotto (2008) and Eckel and Neary (2010)). The top tier contains a continuum of industries of unit measure producing differentiated industry goods packaged together into final goods for consumption. In the bottom tier, each industry has an endogenous mass of superstars and small firms producing differentiated products in a mixed market framework (e.g., Shimomura and Thisse (2012)).We assume that superstars are oligopolistic competitive firms within each industry that compete on prices. Each superstar firm has a positive mass so that its actions affect industry demand. We assume that small firms constitute a monopolistically competitive fringe within each industry. Each small firm is infinitesimal and, therefore, has a negligible impact individually, but their collective decisions affect industry prices.Our model endogenizes the industry market structure by allowing for the entry of new small firms and the transition of some small firms to superstars. A new small firm can enter an industry by paying an upfront startup cost. A small firm can invest in speculative R&D each period, which increases the probability that the small firm becomes a superstar firm. Superstars can increase their likelihood of survival by investing in strategic R&D. Households accumulate intangible capital by investing in fundamental R&D, rented only to superstar firms. The use of intangible capital in production by superstar firms is assumed to generate positive aggregate knowledge spillovers across firms in the economy, leading to sustained trend growth.2.1. Product demandA competitive representative final goods firm produces final goods by first packaging superstar and small firm products into industry goods and then combining the industry goods into final goods. A constant elasticity of substitution (CES) aggregator with unit elasticity bundles together a continuum of differentiated industry goods, Yjt, on a unit measure, j∈[0,1] to produce the final goods, Ȳt: (1)lnȲt=∫01lnYjtdj.The industry good j is constructed by combining the superstar composite (Xjt) and small firm composite (xjt) with a CES aggregator: (2)Yjt=Υ⋅Xjtν−1ν+xjtν−1ννν−1, where Υ>0 determines the relative preference for superstars, and ν>0 is the elasticity of substitution between the two composite goods. The two composite goods bundle the products of the superstars and small firms within industry j according to the following CES aggregators: (3)Xjt=∑i=0NjtXijtν−1ννν−1andxjt=∫0njtxmjtν−1νdmνν−1,respectively, where the elasticity of substitution is given by ν. The number Njt is the endogenous measure of superstar firms industry j, each with a positive mass. The superstars are large oligopolistic firms whose individual actions impact industry demand. The number njt corresponds to the endogenous measure of small firms that constitute the monopolistic competitive fringe in industry j. Each small firm is infinitesimal, whose individual actions are assumed to have a negligible effect, but the small firms collectively impact the industry price.The profit maximization problem of the representative final goods firm yields the demand schedule for superstar i and small firm m in industry j: (4)Θjt(Pijt)≡ȲtΥPjtν−1PijtΥ−νandθjt(pmjt)≡ȲtPjtν−1pmjt−ν,respectively, where Pijt is the price of a superstar firm product i in industry j, pmjt represents the price of a small firm product m in industry j, and Pjt corresponds to the price index of industry j, all of which are defined in terms of the numeraire (final goods). Details of the final goods firm’s problem are contained in internet appendix A.1.2.2. Industry competitionEach industry features superstar and small firm subsectors. Superstars are “large” within the industry but “small” relative to the overall economy (e.g., Jaimovich and Floetotto (2008) and Eckel and Neary (2010)), which is a tractable way of incorporating strategic interactions among firms in general equilibrium. The individual actions of superstars, therefore, impact industry prices but not aggregate factor prices. We assume that the superstars are oligopolists who compete on the prices of differentiated products. The superstars account for strategic interactions among superstars, the collective actions of the small firms within the industry, and the aggregate states of the world. In contrast, the small firms are “small” in the industry and, therefore, not strategic. They produce differentiated products, but they take the industry price index as given when setting prices in a monopolistically competitive setting.We feature the endogenous entry of entrepreneurs into an industry as a small firm and the endogenous transition of some small firms to superstar firms. Entry as a small firm requires paying an upfront fixed startup cost. Small firms spend on speculative R&D, increasing their probability of becoming superstars next period. Superstars use resources on strategic R&D, increasing the probability of survival into the next period.All firms hire labor and rent physical capital from the household sector in competitive factor markets, but only superstar firms can rent intangible capital (e.g., blueprints of new innovations) from the household sector. The private use of the intangible capital in superstar production is assumed to generate aggregate knowledge spillovers, which is standard in endogenous growth models (e.g., Aghion and Howitt (1992) and Grossman and Helpman (1993)).In the ensuing discussion of the superstar and small firm problems, we suppress the industry subscript j for notational convenience. We use uppercase for superstar variables and lowercase for small firm ones.2.2.1. Superstar firmsSuperstar firm i∈[0,Nt] has access to the following technology to produce a differentiated superstar product Xit: (5)Xit=KitαĀtZitηZ̄t1−ηLit1−αξ,where Kit is the capital stock input, Zit is the intangible capital input, Lit is the labor input, Āt is an aggregate technology shock, and Z̄t is the aggregate superstar intangible capital stock.4 The log aggregate technology stock (āt≡logĀt) evolves as: (6)āt=(1−ρ)a⋆+ρāt−1+σϵt,where ϵt∼iidN(0,1). The conditional death probability of a superstar is: (7)Δn,it=Δ0−H(Jit),where Δ0>0 is a constant denoting the average death rate of superstar firms in the absence of strategic R&D and H(Jit) is the endogenous component affecting the conditional death rate next period, specified as an exponential distribution function: (8)H(Jit)=1−e−1ΘJitJ̄t,where Θ>0 is the scale parameter of the exponential distribution and J̄t≡∫0nJitdi. An interpretation of superstars’ strategic innovation is that it relates to the acquisition of potential new patents to eliminate competition before the product is marketable (e.g., “killer innovations” in the sense of Cunningham et al. (2021)) and Bao and Eeckhout (2023)).Each superstar understands how their pricing decisions impact the price index of their industry. The static profit maximization problem arises from the assumption that superstars rent capital from households and, therefore, do not make any intertemporal decisions. Instead, we assume that households accumulate capital stocks. We make this assumption that superstars rent capital as a tractable way to incorporate strategic interactions in a dynamic general equilibrium model, as in Jaimovich and Floetotto (2008) and Corhay et al. Their individual pricing decisions, therefore, impact the industry price index but not the aggregate factor prices. The aggregate actions of superstars across industries, however, do impact the overall economy. Note that a drop in the demand for intangible capital of an individual superstar firm reduces productivity within and across industries due to the aggregate spillover effect from using intangible capital in production. Changes in the market power of superstars, therefore, have long-term effects on trend growth in our model.2.2.2. Small firmsSmall firm m∈[0,nt] has access to the following technology to produce a differentiated small good product xmt: (14)xmt=kmtαĀtZ̄tlmt1−α,where kmt is the capital stock, lmt is the labor input, Āt is the exogenous aggregate technology shock, Z̄t is the aggregate superstar intangible capital stock that determines aggregate trend growth. Endogenous industry structureWe assume that superstar and small firms make simultaneous pricing and production decisions. We focus on a mixed market equilibrium where superstars make identical decisions (Pit=Pt, Lit=Lt, Kit=Kt, Jit=Jt, and Zit=Zt, ∀i) and small firms make identical decisions (pmt=pt, lmt=lt, kmt=kt, and jmt=jt, ∀m).The measure of small firms and superstar firms within each industry is endogenously determined. There is a competitive fringe of startups that can enter into an industry as a small firm by paying an upfront fixed cost, ftE≡κk̄t. The entry cost parameter κ could capture government regulations affecting entrepreneurship. The law of motion for the measure of small firms in an industry is given by: (20)nt+1=(1−δn)(nt+ntE−NtE),where ntE is the entry of new small firms, NtE is the mass of small firms that make a breakthrough innovation to become a superstar next period, and δn is the fraction of randomly chosen small firms (including new entrants this period and small firms that are transitioning to a superstar firm next period) that exit at the end of the period.The free entry condition that pins down the number of new entrants (ntE) equates the expected benefits of entering the industry to the costs: (21)(1−δn)Et[M̄t+1vt+1]=ftE,where vt is the market value of a small firm in the symmetric equilibrium. The aggregate stock of superstar physical capital (K̄t+1) and small firm capital (k̄t+1) evolve as: (25)K̄t+1=(1−δk)K̄t+GkĪt/K̄tK̄tandk̄t+1=(1−δk)k̄t+Gkīt/k̄tk̄t,respectively, where δk is the depreciation rate of physical capital, Īt represents the aggregate investment in superstar physical capital, īt is the aggregate investment in small firm physical capital, and Gk(⋅) captures capital adjustment costs. The aggregate stock of superstar intangible capital (Z̄t+1) follows: (26)Z̄t+1=(1−δz)Z̄t+GzS̄t/Z̄tZ̄t,where δz is the obsolescence rate of intangible capital, S̄t represents the aggregate fundamental R&D investment in intangible capital, and Gz(⋅) captures adjustment costs in R&D. We assume that the functional form for the adjustment costs follows: (27)Gj(x)=α1j1−1ζjx1−1ζj+α2jfor j=k,z as in Jermann (1998). The parameters, α1j and α2j, are chosen so there are no adjustment costs in the deterministic steady state.The household takes prices and aggregate states as given when maximizing lifetime utility by choosing aggregate consumption, investment in the stock of superstar physical capital, investment in the stock of small firm physical capital, fundamental R&D investment in the stock of intangible capital, and labor supply subject to the budget constraint: (28)C̄t+Īt+īt+S̄t+f̄tE=Π̄t+π̄t+K̄tR̄tk+Z̄tR̄tz+k̄tr̄tk+W̄tL̄t,where Π̄t and π̄t represent the aggregate profits paid by superstar and small firms, respectively, and f̄tE represents the aggregate resources devoted to new firm entry. We relegate the details of the household’s problem and the corresponding optimality conditions to internet appendix A.4.The equilibrium stochastic discount factor is given by: (29)M̄t+1=βŪt+1Et(Ūt+11−θ)11−θ−θC̄t+1C̄t−1ψ,where the first term, involving the continuation utility, captures sensitivity regarding uncertainty about long-run growth prospects.2.5. Aggregation and equilibriumAs there is no heterogeneity across industries, we can express aggregate variables in the symmetric equilibrium as Z̄t=NtZt, S̄t=NtSt, K̄t=NtKt, Īt=NtIt, X̄t=NtXt, Π̄t=NtΠt, J̄t≡NtJˆt, k̄t=ntkt, īt=ntit, j̄t=ntjt, π̄t=ntπt, L̄t=NtLt+ntlt, and (30)Ȳt=NtΥ⋅Xtν−1ν+ntxtν−1νν/(ν−1).The aggregate resource constraint is given by: (31)Ȳt=C̄t+Īt+īt+S̄t+J̄t+j̄t+f̄tE,where j̄t represents the aggregate resources devoted to breakthrough innovation and f̄tE=ntEftE are the aggregate fixed entry costs.7The equilibrium is defined as a sequence of prices and quantities such that the following conditions are satisfied: (i) the representative household maximizes lifetime utility, (ii) superstars maximize individual profits, (iii) small firms maximize the present value of current and future profits, and (iv) entry of new small firms satisfies the free entry condition.2.6. Q DecompositionThis section provides a decomposition of Tobin’s Q in the spirit of Hayashi and Inoue (1991) and Crouzet and Eberly (2023) to illuminate the economic margins generating the increasing polarization in Q between superstars and the rest of the firms in the industry documented in Fig. 1. We define our primary measure of Tobin’s Q used in our quantitative analysis as the ratio of the ex-dividend market value to the book value of physical capital.Key margins driving polarization in Q are differences in markups and intangibles between superstars and small firms. Our model produces time variation in markup differences due to superstars being strategic players, implying that their markups endogenously depend on their relative market share. Economic forces affecting superstars’ market share (e.g., changes in relative entry costs or taste parameters), therefore, generate variation in superstar markups. In contrast, small firms are not strategic, with markups that only depend on the demand elasticity and are independent of changes in industry market structure. An increasing superstar market share drives a more significant gap between the markups of superstars and small firms. Superstars also have access to productive intangible capital as a factor of production that generates higher sales. In contrast, intangibles for small firms are expensed without directly contributing to production. For small firms, intangibles can be viewed as a lottery ticket that gives winners the opportunity to be a superstar firm in the next period. A higher intangible capital factor share in superstar production boosts superstar valuations relative to physical capital compared to small firms.A hypothetical claim to the profits of a particular superstar firm has a present value defined recursively as: (32)Vt≡Πt+(1−Δnt)Et[M̄t+1Vt+1].We define the aggregate dividend to the superstar sector as the aggregate profits of incumbent superstars minus the expected costs of purchasing new superstars created during this period plus the net rental income from physical and intangible capital: (33)D̄t≡Π̄t−NS̄t+(R̄tkK̄t−Īt)+(R̄tzZ̄t−S̄t),where NS̄t≡NtE(1−Δnt)Et[M̄t+1Vt+1] is the value of newly created superstars. This definition of the aggregate superstar dividend represents the net transfer of resources from the superstar sector to the households. The ex-dividend value of the claim to the aggregate dividend to the superstar sector, V̄tex, is defined recursively as: (34)V̄tex≡Et[M̄t+1(D̄t+1+V̄t+1ex)].We define Tobin’s Q for the aggregate superstar sector as the ratio of the ex-dividend market value to the book value of physical capital, Q̄t≡V̄tex/K̄t+1. We show in internet appendix B.1 that Tobin’s Q for the superstar sector in the steady state can be expressed as: (35)Q̄=1+Z̄K̄+Π̄/K̄R̄+Δ̄n−Ḡ,where Z̄/K̄ is the average ratio of superstar intangible capital to physical capital, Π̄/K̄ is the average superstar profit to physical capital ratio, R̄ is the average discount rate for superstars, Δ̄n is the average death rate of superstars, and Ḡ is the average growth rate of superstars.8 We focus on the steady-state relation because we are interested in decomposing the long-run trends in Q.We can also express aggregate superstar profits in terms of superstar markups (Φ̄) at the steady state according to Π̄=1−1Φ̄P̄X̄−FN̄Z̄. We can then rewrite Eq. (35) in terms of markups: (36)Q̄=1+Z̄K̄+1−1Φ̄SK̄−FC̄R̄+Δ̄n−Ḡwhere SK̄≡P̄X̄/K̄ is the ratio of superstar sales to physical capital and FC̄≡FN̄Z̄/K̄ is the ratio of superstar fixed costs to physical capital. This expression illustrates how the rising trend in Tobin’s Q of superstar firms can be attributed to an increasing share of intangible capital relative to physical capital, rising markups, increasing sales, falling discount rates, declining exit rates, or higher growth.We define the aggregate dividend of the small firm sector as the aggregate profits of incumbent small firms minus the costs of purchasing new small firms created during this period (net of those becoming superstars) plus the expected benefits of small firms successfully transitioning as superstars, plus net small firm capital rental income: (37)d̄t≡π̄t−ns̄t+eb̄t+(r̄tkk̄t−īt),where ns̄t≡(ntE−NtE)(1−δn)EtM̄t+1vt+1 is the value of newly created small firms this period and eb̄t≡ntΓ(jmt)(1−Δnt)EtM̄t+1Vt+1−(1−δn)EtM̄t+1vt+1 is the aggregate expected benefit of small firms that will successfully become a superstar next period conditional on the level of speculative R&D, jt. This definition of the aggregate small firm dividend represents the net transfer of resources from the small firm sector to the households. The ex-dividend value of the claim to the aggregate dividend to the small firm sector, v̄tex, is defined recursively as: (38)v̄tex≡Et[M̄t+1(d̄t+1+v̄t+1ex)].We define the aggregate Tobin’s Q for the small firm sector as the ex-dividend market value divided by the physical capital stock, Q̄t=v̄tex/k̄t+1. We show in internet appendix B.2 that Tobin’s Q of small firms in the steady state can be expressed as: (39)Q̄=1+vz̄k̄+π̄/k̄r̄+δn−ḡ,where vz̄/k̄ is the average ratio of the present value of net benefits from speculative R&D to physical capital, π̄/k̄ is the average small firm profitability to capital ratio, r̄ is the small firm discount rate, and ḡ is the average small firm growth rate of cash flows.We can write aggregate small firm profits in terms of markups in the steady state as π̄=(1−1φ̄)p̄x̄−j̄−fn̄Z̄. We also define the aggregate value of intangible capital in the small firm sector as the difference between the present value of the aggregate benefits from speculative R&D and the present value of the aggregate investment expenditures in speculative R&D, z̄≡vz̄−j̄/(r̄+δn−ḡ). Substituting the identity for profits and the definition of z̄ in Eq. (39) yields: (40)Q̄t=1+z̄k̄+1−1φ̄sk̄−fc̄r̄+δn−ḡ,where z̄/k̄ is the average ratio of small firm intangible capital to physical capital, sk̄≡p̄x̄/k̄ is the average ratio of small firm sales to physical capital, fc̄≡fn̄Z̄/k̄ is the average ratio of aggregate small firm fixed costs to physical capital. Additional details of the derivation are contained in internet appendix B.2.The sources of polarization between the aggregate Tobin’s Q of superstars and small firms can, therefore, be viewed through the lens of the decomposition: (41)Q̄−Q̄=Z̄K̄−z̄k̄+1−1Φ̄SK̄−FC̄R̄+Δ̄n−Ḡ−1−1φ̄sk̄−fc̄r̄+δn−ḡ.This equation highlights how the increasing superstar Q relative to the small firm Q can be attributed to an increasing share of intangible capital, rising markups, accelerating growth, increasing profitability, falling fixed costs, declining discount rates, or declining exit rates of superstars relative to small firms. Fig. 1 suggests that the primary sources of the increasing polarization in Q are attributed to growing differences in the markups and intangible capital share. Each of these margins in the decomposition is endogenous and, therefore, interrelated. We quantify the role of these margins for explaining the patterns observed in Fig. 1 using our estimated model in the next section.We can also compare Tobin’s Q and P/E ratios in the steady state to elucidate potential economic channels for explaining why there is increasing polarization in Tobin’s Q between superstars and small firms but not P/E ratios, illustrated in Figure A.2 from the internet appendix. Since all the earnings and benefits from owning the claims to the superstar and small firm sectors are distributed to households, earnings are equal to dividends. The aggregate P/E ratio for superstars (PĒ≡V̄ex/D̄) and small firms (pē≡v̄ex/d̄) at the steady state are: (42)PĒ=1R̄−Ḡandpē=1r̄−ḡ.The P/E ratios are only indirectly affected by the markup and intangible capital margins through the endogenous discount rate and growth rate terms in the denominator, while Tobin’s Q ratios directly depend on the markup and the intangible capital share. Fig. 1 illustrated how growth and discount rates remained similar between superstars and small firms while markups and intangible capital diverged strongly. Through the lens of the Q and P/E decompositions, these empirical trends are consistent with the rising polarization in Q within industries but not P/E ratios. Section 5.2 formalizes this interpretation in our model estimation and quantitatively links the decomposition to shifts in the structural parameters.
article.3. Data: This section describes the data series and the construction of empirical proxies used in the quantitative evaluation of our model.3.1. Macroeconomic dataQuarterly data for per capita consumption and output are obtained from the Bureau of Economic Analysis (BEA). Consumption is measured as the sum of services and nondurable goods consumption. Output is measured as real gross domestic product. The aggregate stock of physical capital is computed as the sum of non-residential structures and equipment, while intangible capital sums non-residential software and R&D. Both series for the aggregate stock and investment in the two types of capital are from the BEA.The labor share is defined as the ratio of the total compensation of employees to the gross value added for the non-financial corporate business sector and is available from the BEA. Labor hours are obtained from the Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) and measured as the average weekly hours of production within the private sector. Aggregate productivity is measured using the utilization-adjusted total factor productivity method, as developed by Fernald (2014). The aggregate death rate is defined as the total number of firms’ closures in a year, divided by the total number of operating firms from the previous year. Information about firm dynamics, including total operating firms and closures, comes from the Business Dynamics Statistics (BDS) database.Aggregate financial data is obtained from CRSP. The real one-year interest rate is calculated by subtracting realized inflation (measured by the year-on-year change in the consumer price index) from the one-year Treasury yield. The dividend yield is computed following Campbell and Shiller (1988) by dividing the total dividends paid over the past year by S&P 500 firms by the current market value of the securities.Internet appendix C provides additional details pertaining to these data series, including the data sources.3.2. Microeconomic dataFirm-level accounting variables are sourced from Compustat. Firm revenue is measured by sales (SALE). Firm earnings are defined as net income (NI). The firm-level stock of physical capital is defined as the gross value of property, plant, and equipment (PPEGT). The stock of intangible capital is measured according to Peters and Taylor (2017) and is directly obtained from the Wharton Research Data Service (K_INT). Investment in physical capital is defined as capital expenditures (CAPEX). Investment in intangible capital is measured following Peters and Taylor (2017) as reported R&D expenses (XRD) plus 30% of selling, general & administrative expenses (XSGA). The market value of the firm’s equity is defined as the price per share (PRCC_F) multiplied by the number of shares outstanding (CSHO). Tobin’s Q is measured as the sum of debt value (DLTT+DLC-ACT) plus equity value, divided by the total stock of physical capital. Profitability is defined as the return on physical capital (ROK), obtained by dividing net income by physical capital (NI/PPEGT).Superstar firms are identified as those within the top 5th percentile of the market capitalization distribution within a given year and industry.9 Small firms comprise the firms falling within the bottom 95th percentile. Industries are defined by the four-digit North American Industry Classification System (NAICS) code. Firm-level variables are aggregated at the small and superstar levels by summing the corresponding variables across all firms belonging to each respective group.3.3. Markup constructionWe construct our price markup measure using the production approach pioneered by Hall (1988). This method derives firm-level markups by leveraging the cost-minimization principle of a variable input within production, which is a necessary condition for firms’ problems in our model. A notable advantage of this approach is its capability to derive the price markup without necessitating the specification of a particular demand system.Under the assumptions that (i) the production function is differentiable and (ii) factor prices are taken as given, a firm minimizing costs equates the marginal cost of each production input to the marginal product. We can then rearrange the first order conditions with respect to any variable production inputs (as depicted in Eqs. (13), (19)), we can express the firm’s price markup as follows: (43)markupit=ξitv/shareitv,where ξitv represents the output elasticity of variable input v, and shareitv denotes the proportion of total firm revenues attributed to expenses associated with variable input v. For example, when considering the labor margin, the markup for small firms can be identified by setting ξitl=(1−α) and shareitl=(W̄tlit)/(pitxit).The Compustat database does not provide a breakdown of expenditures by production inputs, such as labor, electricity, etc. Consequently, following the methodology of De Loecker et al. (2020), we employ the reported cost of goods sold (COGS) as the total variable cost for measuring expenditure share. Moreover, we directly source the estimates for production elasticities from the aforementioned paper. Consistent with our model assumptions, these elasticities are derived by estimating a Cobb–Douglas production function on COGS, while accounting for various other production inputs, including capital (PPEGT) and overhead costs (SG&A). The inclusion of overhead costs ensures that the estimation effectively accommodates the documented rising trend in overhead costs, as discussed by Traina (2018). Importantly, these estimates allow for potential variations across industries and time, as the elasticities are estimated within five-year rolling windows, pooling firms at the two-digit NAICS level.10 The aggregate markup for small and superstar firms is computed by COGS-weighting firm-level markups.Table 1. article.4. Estimation and identification: This section presents how we estimate and identify the model parameters for the quantitative evaluation.4.1. EstimationWe estimate the majority of the structural parameters using a simulated method of moments (SMM) approach. The remaining parameters are calibrated and fixed across the subsamples.The thirteen estimated parameters are O′≡[a∗,β,γ,Θ,δn,κ,ϑ,η,Υ, α,F,f,ν]′. The SMM procedure chooses values for Ô to minimize the distance between a vector of identifying moments from the data and the corresponding moments generated from model simulations: (44)Ô=arg minOmˆ−m(O)′Wmˆ−m(O),where W is a weighting matrix, mˆ is a vector of empirical moments, and m(O) is the vector of model-implied moments given a value of O for the structural parameters.11 Although the estimator, Ô, is consistent for any positive-definite weighting matrix, we follow the literature and set W equal to the inverse of the covariance matrix of the moments. This specification allocates more weight to moments estimated with greater precision, and it has the advantage of minimizing the asymptotic variance of Ô. We estimate the covariance matrix of the moments for each subperiod using the influence function approach (e.g., Erickson and Whited (2002)) and convert yearly data to quarterly frequency using linear interpolation when necessary. In addition, we correct for the potential effect of serial correlation across target moments by adjusting our covariance matrix following Newey and West (1987) with three lags.Using thirteen identifying moments (including those exhibiting increasing polarization depicted in Fig. 1) computed in two subperiods (1978–1998 and 1999–2019), we estimate the thirteen parameters (O) for each period. Our main objective is to uncover the sources of the polarization trends in market power and Tobin’s Q. The target moments by period are reported in Panel A of Table 3, the parameter estimates by period with standard errors are tabulated in Panel B, and the calibration of the fixed parameters is presented in Panel C. Standard errors are obtained as the square root of the diagonal elements of the covariance matrix of the parameter estimates, which is obtained following the standard Generalized Method of Moments parameter variance formula, adjusted for simulation error.12 A detailed description of the target moments can be found in internet appendix C.4.2. IdentificationWe use thirteen moments to identify the thirteen estimated parameters by period. Identification requires that the simulated moments are sensitive to variations in the parameter values. Our endogenous growth framework implies that steady-state growth is a function of all structural parameters. The adjustment frictions in our model and competitive environment also make other target moments affected by all parameters in varying degrees. Table 1 presents the Jacobian matrix to illustrate the sensitivity of target moments to a percentage change in a specific estimated parameter. We next characterize how certain target moments can be informative about particular estimated parameters.We use the average small firm markup to identify the elasticity of substitution across products (ν) since this parameter pins down small firm markups in our model (φ=ν/(ν−1)). The average sales share of the superstar sector relative to the industry (NΩ) is informative about the superstar share parameter (Υ) in the aggregator, capturing the relative preference for the composite superstar good. The superstar markup explicitly depends on the elasticity of substitution parameter (pinned down by the small firm markup) and on the market share of an individual superstar in the industry (Ω), as shown in Eq. (12). Since superstars are oligopolists, their market share and markups are sensitive to the number of competitors in the industry. Consequently, the average superstar markup (Φ) is informative about the steady-state number of superstar competitors in an industry (N).The average superstar competitors (N) in an industry are endogenous and depend on parameters affecting entry costs (κ and ϑ) and exit rates (Θ and δn) in both sectors due to the endogenous transitions between and out of these sectors. The average superstar markup can help to identify these four parameters since it is tightly linked to N, as discussed above. As one moment cannot identify four parameters, we need three additional moments to determine these four parameters jointly. The second moment is the average sales ratio between a single superstar and a single small firm, which is informative about the average ratio of the number of small firms to the number of superstar firms (N/n). The ratio N/n depends directly on the parameters ϑ, δn, and Θ in the steady state. The third moment is the average difference in Tobin’s Q between superstars and small firms (Q̄−Q̄). This average difference is highly informative about the parameter inversely affecting the probability that small firms become superstars (ϑ) as seen in Eq. (18). The fourth moment is the average death rate of small firms directly pins down the parameter governing the exit rate of small firms (δn).The average intangible capital share identifies the parameter determining the intangible capital share (η) in the steady state. The average labor share identifies the parameter inversely related to the labor share (α) in the steady state. The average return on capital of superstars and small firms is informative for the fixed operating cost parameters for superstars (F) and small firms (f). The average riskfree rate is inversely related to the subjective discount factor (β). The average earnings-price ratio helps to identify the parameter related to the coefficient of relative risk aversion (γ). The risk aversion parameter affects the average earnings-price ratio through the discount rate. The average growth rate helps to identify the scale parameter (a∗) in the level of productivity.

The scale parameter in the exogenous technology component (a∗) has declined over the two periods, contributing to the slowdown in economic growth over the past few decades. The increasing estimates of the parameter η over our sample reflect the rising share of intangible capital. This pattern relates to the growing importance of the R&D-intensive high-tech sector in the overall economy, highlighted in Alexander and Eberly (2018). Details regarding the definition and construction of variables can be found in internet appendix C.The estimated parameters dictating entry and exit dynamics within and across superstar and small firm sectors (Θ, δn, κ, ϑ) are important for generating industry polarization.13 The decline in the death rate parameter of small firms (δn) and the rising fixed cost of entry into an industry as a small firm (κ) deter entry rates. These two margins possibly capture rising regulations over our sample that have adversely affected business dynamism highlighted in Gutiérrez et al. (2021). The increase in the parameter inversely related to the probability that small firms make breakthrough innovations (ϑ) reflects how novel ideas are becoming harder to find (e.g., Bloom et al. (2020) and Cavenaile et al. (2019)). We find that Θ has increased, implying that strategic R&D by superstars relative to the industry (Jit/J̄) is becoming less efficient at improving superstar survival rates. However, the increase in ϑ dampens the crowding out effect, making strategic R&D by superstars relative to the industry more efficient. We find that the increase in ϑ dominates the effect of higher Θ so that the average death rates of superstars (Δ̄n) fall in our sample.The remaining estimated parameters relate to goods demand (ν and Υ) and fixed costs (F and f). The decline in the elasticity of substitution parameter between products (ν) and increasing tastes for superstar products (Υ) are important for jointly explaining the widening gap in markups and sales shares within industries. A fall in the demand elasticity parameter increases superstar markups relative to small firms, but it reduces the sales share of superstars. Neiman and Vavra (2023) find rising heterogeneity in consumption bundles across households over the recent two decades, reflecting an increasing preference for variety in aggregate demand. Clara et al. (2021) show that the increasing demand for variety is mostly supplied by superstar firms expanding firm boundaries. (2020).Panel C of Table 3 presents the calibrated parameters. These changes in Θ and ϑ lead to a decline in Δn, which in turn reduces the exit rate differential (Δn−δn). The estimated changes in both Δn and ϑ make it more difficult for small firms to become superstars within their industry, increasing the market share of individual superstars who are oligopolists within their industry. Superstar markups are increasing with respect to their individual market share within an industry, as shown in Eq. (12). Small firms charge a constant markup since they are atomistic within their industry and do not face frictions in adjusting prices. This entry risk propagation mechanism of oligopolists is similar to Corhay et al. (2020). The discount rates of small firms are not affected as much by changes in market shares since they have constant markups. (42) showed that P/E ratios in the steady state only depend on discount rates and growth rates, while Q also depends on markups, sales to capital, and intangibles, which are the key margins driving polarization in Q. We showed in the context of the Q decomposition (Table 5) that our model matches the observations from Figure A.2 in the internet appendix, showing that growth and discount rates did not exhibit increasing polarization, implying that P/E ratios would not diverge in our model estimation. Indeed, the change in the P/E differential (PĒ−pē) across the two periods generated by our model is close to zero.Our Q decomposition illuminates sources of rising inequality within industries. Our estimates suggest the importance of higher barriers to entry for both startups to become small firms and for small firms to become superstars. Declining competitiveness at both stages in the life cycle has contributed to and reinforced the increasing polarization in market power between superstars and small firms in the last four decades. However, the rising barriers of entry at the early stage (startups becoming small firms) were far more important for creating inequality in Tobin’s Q compared to the rising barriers at the later stage (small firms becoming superstars).The reason why the entry barriers at different stages of firms have heterogeneous effects on Q is that the rising barriers for startups to become small firms increase the returns to speculative innovation by small firms. In contrast, the rising barriers for small firms to become superstars decrease returns to speculative innovation. More speculative innovation increases the number of small firms that make successful breakthrough innovations, increasing the mass of superstars. A larger mass of superstars raises aggregate productivity and increases aggregate knowledge spillovers, leading to increased superstar production and higher aggregate growth. Higher aggregate growth magnifies the differences in markups and sales on Q between superstars and small firms (by making the denominator smaller in the decomposition from Eq. (45)). Rising superstar barriers decrease aggregate growth and compress the dispersion in sales, dampening the impact of the differences in markups on Q.The results in this section highlight how pro-competitive policies encouraging the creation of new firms and those curbing the market power of superstars can have different implications for inequality within industries. Policy discussionThis section qualitatively considers the impact of surprise policy changes on the entry barriers for a startup to become a small firm (κ, δn) and for small firms to become superstars (ϑ, Θ) separately. We extend our benchmark model by introducing shocks to each of these entry barrier parameters, modeled as autoregressive processes.15 We interpret these shocks as a reduced-form way of capturing surprise regulatory actions targeting new business creation (shocks to κ and δn) or the market power of superstars directly (shocks to ϑ and Θ). The Q decomposition above highlighted the differential impact of these entry margins on valuations despite both contributing significantly to markup polarization. We now focus on how pro-competitive policies can have very different implications depending on the source.Fig. 2 compares shocks to ϑ and κ that are each calibrated to produce a 1% drop in the superstar markup (Φ) to facilitate comparison. Lowering the barriers for small firms to become superstars (through lower ϑ) reduces the market share of individual superstars within an industry, leading to reduced superstar markups. The lower barriers to becoming a superstar have two opposing effects on the incentive for small firms to engage in speculative innovation. First, lower ϑ reduces superstar rents and depresses the expected superstar valuations relative to small firms (E(V−v)), decreasing the expected benefits of speculative innovation conditional on success. Second, lower ϑ also increases the marginal return of speculative innovation on the probability of success. The second effect dominates so that relative speculative innovation (j/j̄) goes up, increasing the success rate of small firms transitioning as new superstars (nE/n). More superstars raise aggregate productivity by enhancing aggregate knowledge spillovers in the production technology. Higher productivity increases factor demands for superstar intangible capital and reinforces the effect of lower markups on factor demands. The increase in factor demands leads to more fundamental innovation (s), raising expected growth (E(Δy)).Lowering the barriers for startups to enter an industry as a small firm (through lower κ) increases the mass of small firms, decreasing the relative market share of individual superstars, translating to reduced superstar markups (Φ). Reducing superstar rents lowers their valuations relative to small firms (E(V−v)), decreasing the incentives for small firms to engage in speculative innovation. More small firms exacerbate the crowding out effect, further discouraging speculative innovation. A lower relative speculative innovation rate (j/j̄) reduces the success rate of small firms becoming new superstars (nE/n). Fewer superstars depress aggregate productivity by lowering the aggregate accumulation of intangible capital, which reduces knowledge spillovers in production technology. Lower productivity, in turn, decreases factor demands for superstar intangible capital and dominates the offsetting effects of lower markups on factor demands, leading to less fundamental innovation (s) in the aggregate and lower expected growth (E(Δy)).Figure A.3 in the internet appendix compares the impulse response functions for shocks to Θ and δn, where the size of the shock is also set to generate a 1% decline in superstar markups. The qualitative effects on growth are similar to the shocks to ϑ and κ. Lowering exit rates for superstars (Δn) via a shock to Θ increases growth, but lowering exit rates for small firms (δn) decreases growth. These results highlight the stark aggregate consequences of pro-competitive policies targeting large versus small firms when there are endogenous firm transitions in mixed market industries. These competition policies distort incentives for innovation that are the engine of growth. Lowering the entry barriers to becoming a superstar raises long-term growth, but reducing entry barriers to create new small firms has the opposite effect on growth.
article.6. Conclusion: We document increasing polarization in Tobin’s Q within industries between superstars and the rest of the firms over the past four decades. The widening gap in Q mirrors the growing inequality in markups, sales, and intangible capital among firms. We endogenously link these industry polarization trends using a general equilibrium model of mixed industries comprising superstars and small firms, estimated separately in each half of the sample. We quantify the sources of polarization in Q using a structural decomposition that relates Q to markups, sales, intangible capital, discount rates, and growth rates, where the driving forces of these margins are through the estimated changes in the deep parameters of the model.Our model estimation suggests that the parameters producing rising barriers to becoming small firms, increasing factor demand for superstar intangible capital, and rising demand for superstar goods contributed equally to the increasing polarization in Q. These margins also generated growing inequality in markups, sales, and intangible capital shares, but not in discount rates and growth rates like in the data. While rising barriers to becoming small firms and superstars both contribute to a widening gap in markups, only the small firm entry barriers have significant effects on inequality in Q due to how these barriers distort innovation incentives and trend growth in opposite directions. Q polarization is, therefore, an informative moment about the sources of industry inequality.We explore how surprise changes to entry barriers at the superstar and small firm stages separately to examine the effects of pro-competitive policies on industry inequality and aggregate growth. Our policy experiments suggest that directly curbing the market power of superstars by lowering superstar barriers reduces polarization in markups but not in Q while stimulating innovation incentives and boosting trend growth. Lowering small firm barriers reduces inequality in markups and Q but dampens innovation incentives and lowers trend growth. These experiments highlight the importance of incorporating a mixed industry structure for competition policy.
